quote: And then while I pull that up, what kinda got you, you know, looking at solar for the home?
stewart_read: Parker asks the schema's designated 'EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' question as a multitasking aside — 'while I pull that up' — and spends under 30 seconds on it before moving to the next data point. Mitchell's answer ('the bills I've been getting through the winter months') is a genuine pain signal that Parker acknowledges with 'Yeah. So since getting pretty high on you lately then?' and immediately pivots away. The schema explicitly marks this item as depth-graded and notes top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Parker treats it as a checkbox.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may have been intentionally brief here to build toward the bill-collection moment as the deeper anchor. Rejected — there is no evidence of a later return to the emotional pain; the bill is captured as data, not flipped emotionally.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the 00:40-01:08 segment and ask Parker what he heard Mitchell say. Then ask what he did with it. The gap is not that he asked the question — it's that he didn't stay in it. The schema says this is the differentiator between top and bottom setters. Parker has the instinct to ask; he needs to learn to sit in the answer.

quote: Yeah. I was so happy to get that bill out of the mail today.
stewart_read: This is the call's highest-leverage moment. Mitchell volunteers genuine emotional relief — the furnace is fixed, the bill dropped, and he's happy. Parker responds 'Yeah. For sure.' and moves to 'Definitely see what we can do to save you some money there.' The bill is captured as a data point but never flipped. The schema's capture-pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; Parker delivers 'ask + acknowledge + move on' — the universal failure pattern noted across the corpus.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may have been saving the flip for the appointment framing. Rejected — there is no flip at any point in the call. The emotional relief Mitchell expressed is never converted into alignment-against-the-utility.
coaching_implication: This is the flip opportunity. The coaching move is not to teach Parker a script line — it's to show him that Mitchell's relief is the lever. Suggested direction for the 1-on-1: 'Mitchell told you he was happy the bill went down. What does that tell you about how he feels when it goes back up? That's the crack. Solar doesn't just lower the bill — it removes the anxiety of waiting for the next one.' No dollar figures needed. The emotion is already in the transcript.

quote: With solar, Mitchell, there's a few different programs that we can we'll go over with you. Just to qualify for each of those, you just need to have a credit score above 670. Does that sound like the case for you or your wife?
stewart_read: Parker correctly does not DQ Mitchell on uncertain stated credit. Mitchell responds 'I think that's what it is. I haven't checked it, but I bought this house through the VA with this credit score.' Parker's response — 'Okay. And, yeah, you should be good there.' — passes Mitchell through without a hard DQ on self-reported uncertainty. This is consistent with the schema's spencer_operator_note coaching implication: stated credit is unreliable, and a VA home purchase is a reasonable proxy for creditworthiness. Parker's instinct here is correct.
coaching_implication: Positive moment to name explicitly in the 1-on-1. Parker didn't DQ on 'I think that's what it is' — that's the right call. The VA reference is a legitimate signal. Reinforce this instinct so it becomes deliberate, not accidental.

quote: Text message, sir, because I don't know how to do it on email. I'm a senior citizen, so I don't know all that stuff.
stewart_read: Mitchell self-identifies as a senior citizen with limited tech capability at 03:51. Parker responds 'Yeah. No worries. No worries. I'll text you right now' and proceeds to wait approximately 90 seconds while Mitchell navigates his phone to find, open, and send the bill photo. The schema's elderly_low_tech_protocol specifies: pivot to verbal read-back within 2 minutes of friction, not 10. Mitchell manages to send the photo (05:36), but the wait includes audible confusion ('Hold on one second,' 'Let me open it up,' a side conversation with someone in the room). Parker's 'No worries. Take your time.' is patient but passive — he never offers the verbal read-back alternative.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may have judged that Mitchell could handle the photo send given he'd already received the text. Partially supported — Mitchell does succeed. But the schema's trigger is the self-identification of low-tech capability, not failure to complete the task. The protocol is about efficiency and dignity, not just outcome.
coaching_implication: Play the 03:51-05:36 segment. Ask Parker: 'At what point did you know this might take a while?' Then introduce the verbal read-back pivot. The coaching point is not that Parker failed — Mitchell sent the bill. It's that Parker has a faster, lower-friction path available the moment a customer says 'I'm a senior citizen.' The schema names this explicitly.

quote: He didn't tell me. He said, it was, a grant or something that the state pays. Not me in the state. He said it take four to six months to get approved.
stewart_read: Mitchell references a prior contact — 'he' — who made a specific promise about a state-funded grant with a 4-6 month approval timeline. This is a scam-anxiety-adjacent signal: a prior in-person solar contact made financial promises that Parker cannot verify. Parker's response ('Gotcha. Yeah. We can definitely get that going for you.') implicitly validates the claim without confirming it. This is a scope-discipline failure — Parker is affirming a promise made by an unknown third party about a program Ion may or may not offer in the same form. The schema's setter_scope_discipline universal escape hatch applies: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer.'
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Parker may have been trying to keep momentum and avoid a tangent. Partially supported — the call does move forward. But the implicit validation creates a downstream expectation gap the closer must now manage.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, flag the difference between 'keeping momentum' and 'validating a claim you can't confirm.' Parker's line 'We can definitely get that going for you' sounds like a promise. The correct move is a neutral bridge: 'Our specialist will go through all the program options with you on Friday — they'll know exactly what's available in your area.' No validation, no dismissal, no scope creep.

quote: This is me and my wife.
stewart_read: Mitchell volunteers that his wife lives in the home at 01:53. Parker acknowledges and moves on without asking whether the wife will be on the appointment. The schema's spouse_decision protocol requires that co-decision-makers be included in the appointment — not overcome as an objection, but actively included. Parker never asks whether the wife will be available Friday at 10AM or whether she should be on the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may have judged that Mitchell's confident scheduling ('I ain't got nothing to do Friday') indicated he was the sole decision-maker. Rejected — Mitchell explicitly identified his wife as a co-resident, and the schema's title_co_owners verify item exists precisely to surface this. Parker never asked if she's on the title.
coaching_implication: This is the protocol gap with the highest no-show risk. In the 1-on-1: 'Mitchell told you it's him and his wife. Did you ask if she'd be on the call Friday?' The fix is one question: 'Will your wife be able to join us Friday at ten?' If she can't, reschedule for when she can. The schema is explicit — setters who 'win' this moment lose the deal.

quote: Okay. Perfect. Well, I'll get you down Friday at 10AM. Our specialist will just drop by and and run through that design with you and answer any questions you guys might have.
stewart_read: Parker books the appointment but omits three of the six required button_up elements: (1) the one-hour block statement is absent; (2) the phone-vs-in-person specification is ambiguous — 'drop by' implies in-person, which is a different appointment type than the schema's phone_appointment_specified requirement; (3) the 'is that fair' verbal handshake is absent; (4) the no-driving/at-computer anchor is absent. The text follow-up promise is also not explicitly stated on the call. The booking is real but structurally incomplete.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may have been reading Mitchell's enthusiasm ('I ain't got nothing to do Friday') as sufficient commitment and abbreviated the button-up accordingly. Rejected — the schema's button_up elements exist precisely because enthusiasm at booking does not predict show rate. The 'is that fair' handshake is explicitly noted as predictive of show rate.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through the button_up checklist against this call. He hit datetime confirmation and the specialist framing. He missed the hour-block, the verbal handshake, the no-driving anchor, and the text-follow-up promise. The 'drop by' language also needs attention — if this is a phone appointment, 'drop by' sets a wrong expectation. Ask Parker: 'What does Mitchell think is happening Friday at ten?'

quote: I can know about this thing that he told me about the the grant that the state paid for.
stewart_read: Post-booking, Mitchell returns to the grant promise from the prior contact. This is the second touch of the multi-touch pattern. Parker's response — 'Exactly. Yeah. They'll go over all that with you on Friday and how we take care of the upfront cost and everything like that.' — correctly defers to the closer but the word 'Exactly' again implicitly validates the grant claim. The booking is already confirmed; this is not a conditional booking. The risk is that the closer walks in to a customer expecting a specific state-funded grant program that may not exist as described.
coaching_implication: The closer needs to know about this before Friday. The coaching point for Parker is the word 'Exactly' — it's a validation reflex. The neutral bridge ('Our specialist will walk through all the available programs') does the same job without creating an expectation the closer has to walk back.
